content:
{
  "title": "Adversarially Regularized Prompt Ensembles for Robust Language Model Alignment",
  "problem": "Large language models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that circumvent safety constraints. Current prompting methods insufficiently generalize to unseen or adaptive attack strategies.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs proliferate across sensitive domains, ensuring robustness to adversarial manipulation becomes urgent. Existing methods often rely on static prompt templates or single-instance prompt engineering, which fail against adaptive and distribution-shift attacks. Exploring dynamic, ensemble-based prompting could provide stronger, more generalizable defenses without fine-tuning model parameters.",
  "method": "We propose a prompting method that generates an ensemble of paraphrased prompts for each user input, where paraphrases are induced by a controlled, adversarial regularization process. Specifically, for a target instruction, we create multiple semantically equivalent paraphrases using a paraphraser trained to maximize diversity while preserving intent, and pass all to the LLM independently. The LLM's outputs are then aggregated via a consensus or voting mechanism, with global constraints enforced when discrepancies arise. During inference, adversarial paraphrasers can also simulate potential attack prompts, enabling real-time detection and mitigation of jailbreak attempts through disagreement analysis and output gating.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on jailbreak/adversarial prompt datasets such as AdvBench and jailbreak subsets of AlpacaEval. Baselines include standard single-prompt and static template prompting. Metrics cover attack success rate, agreement rates among ensemble members, and utility retention on standard instructions. Ablation experiments will vary paraphrase diversity and aggregation strategies."
}
